sourceText: This emphasizes that rather than seeking external solutions, one should manifest their own inner light and wisdom.

Ibn Rushd faced severe opposition from religious authorities due to his philosophical ideas and his commentaries on Aristotle. His writings were deemed heretical, banned, his books burned, and he suffered exile.

His ideas were secretly preserved and transmitted to European scholars, profoundly influencing the intellectual development of the Renaissance. His rational approach laid a crucial foundation for later philosophical and scientific advancements.

The seeker of truth belongs to one of three classes: the man who seeks truth through faith, the man who seeks truth through reason, and the man who seeks truth through the senses. Reason alone is the most certain path to truth.
Ibn Rushd believed that seeking truth through reason was the most reliable method.
All human endeavors are ultimately aimed at the pursuit of happiness.
He viewed all human activities as directed towards the ultimate goal of happiness.
Knowledge makes us better, and understanding makes us stronger.
Emphasizes the essential role of acquiring knowledge and deep understanding for personal growth and development.

Suffered from severe depression and neurasthenia in his youth, experiencing identity confusion due to multiple career changes. Also faced internal conflict in finding his own path amidst his father's philosophical influence and constant self-reflection.

Overcame his struggles by thoroughly analyzing his painful experiences and emphasizing the importance of 'will' and 'action.' He maintained an active attitude towards life, developing philosophical and psychological insights through experience and practice.
Beliefs are the basis of action.
What we believe forms the basis of our actions, and positive beliefs lead to positive outcomes.
